era_context: The Western Roman Empire is under severe military and political pressure. Alaric I and his Visigothic forces are besieging Rome, having marched on the city in 408. Negotiations between Alaric and Emperor Honorius are ongoing but repeatedly break down. In Britain, Roman authority is collapsing as troops are withdrawn to defend Italy. In Hispania and Gaul, the usurper Constantine III controls much of the western provinces. The Eastern Roman Empire under Theodosius II remains more stable but is increasingly independent of the West.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond the Rhine (sparse documentation)
East Asia (contemporary Chinese sources may exist but are underrepresented)

events.title: Alaric I's siege of Rome continues; negotiations with Honorius
events.description: Alaric I maintained his siege of Rome throughout 409, applying economic pressure by cutting off grain supplies from North Africa. Negotiations with Emperor Honorius continued but repeatedly failed. Alaric demanded land, money, and recognition; Honorius was unwilling to grant these terms. The city's population faced starvation, and the Senate was forced to negotiate directly with Alaric. At one point, the Senate attempted to install a rival emperor (Attalus) to negotiate with Alaric, but this arrangement collapsed. The siege would continue into 410, culminating in the sack of Rome in August of that year.
key_figures: Priscus Attalus (Roman senator, briefly installed as rival emperor)

sources.name: Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans
Augustine of Hippo, City of God (written 413–426, but referencing events of 409–410)

events.certainty_note: The siege and negotiations are attested in multiple sources, but precise chronology within 408–410 is uncertain. Zosimus and Orosius are writing decades later, and Augustine is writing after the sack. No contemporary written records from the siege itself survive.

events.title: Vandals, Alans, and Sueves cross into Hispania
events.description: In 409, a coalition of Germanic and Sarmatian peoples—Vandals (Hasding and Silingi branches), Alans, and Sueves—crossed the Pyrenees into Hispania, likely invited or tolerated by the usurper Constantine III as foederati (allied troops). These groups had been operating in Gaul and were seeking new territory. They rapidly overran much of Hispania, establishing themselves in various regions: the Vandals in the south and southeast, the Alans in the center, and the Sueves in the northwest. This marked the beginning of the end of Roman Hispania and the establishment of barbarian kingdoms that would dominate the peninsula for centuries.
key_figures: Constantine III (Usurper, possibly facilitating the crossing)

sources.name: Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans
Archaeological evidence: settlement patterns, material culture

events.certainty_note: The crossing into Hispania is attested in multiple sources, but the exact date is debated. Hydatius places it in 409; some modern scholars argue for 406–407. The date 409 is conventional but not certain. The scale and nature of the 'invasion' are also debated—it may have been a gradual settlement rather than a sudden military conquest.
cross_references: Part of broader Germanic migration into Roman territory (406–409)
Led to establishment of Vandal, Alan, and Suevic kingdoms in Hispania

events.title: Constantine III's authority in Gaul and Hispania reaches its peak
events.description: By 409, Constantine III, the usurper proclaimed emperor in Britain in 407, had consolidated control over much of Gaul and Hispania. He ruled from Arles and maintained the apparatus of imperial government, including the minting of coins and the appointment of officials. However, his authority was increasingly challenged by barbarian movements (Vandals, Alans, Sueves in Hispania; Visigoths under Alaric in Italy) and by the legitimate Western Roman Emperor Honorius. Constantine III's regime represented a fragmentation of imperial authority and would begin to collapse in 410–411.
key_figures: Gerontius (Roman general, initially supporting Constantine III)

sources.name: Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans
Numismatic evidence: coins minted in Constantine III's name

events.certainty_note: Constantine III's rule in Gaul and Hispania is well attested in numismatic and literary sources. The extent of his authority and the degree of his control are debated, but his existence and rule are confirmed.

events.title: Theodosius II consolidates power in the Eastern Roman Empire
events.description: In 409, Theodosius II, who had become emperor in 408 at age seven, was gradually consolidating power with the help of his sister Pulcheria and the praetorian prefect Anthemius. The Eastern Roman Empire remained administratively intact and militarily stronger than the West, though it faced its own challenges from Huns and other barbarian groups on its northern frontier. Theodosius II would reign until 450 and would be responsible for the Theodosian Code, a major legal compilation. The Eastern Empire's stability contrasted sharply with the Western Empire's collapse.

sources.name: Theodosius II's legislation and edicts (preserved in the Theodosian Code)

events.certainty_note: Theodosius II's reign and the role of Pulcheria and Anthemius are well documented in contemporary legislation and near-contemporary chronicles.
cross_references: Part of broader Eastern Roman stability in the late 4th and early 5th centuries

disconfirming_evidence: The narrative of the 'barbarian invasions' of Hispania in 409 has been significantly revised by modern scholarship. Rather than a sudden military conquest, the crossing of Vandals, Alans, and Sueves into Hispania may have been a gradual settlement process, possibly facilitated by Roman authorities (Constantine III) seeking military allies. The scale of the 'invasion' is also debated—it may have involved far fewer people than traditional sources suggest. Additionally, the degree of disruption to Roman Hispania is uncertain; some regions may have continued functioning under Roman or Romano-barbarian administration for decades after 409. The traditional narrative of 'invasion and conquest' obscures the complexity of late antique settlement and integration.
historiographic_note: The year 409 is moderately well documented for events in the Mediterranean and Gaul, but sources are unevenly distributed. Zosimus and Orosius are the primary sources for Alaric's siege and Constantine III's rule, but both are writing decades later. Hydatius is the primary source for the crossing into Hispania, but his chronicle is also written later (5th century). Contemporary written records are sparse; most information comes from later compilations and chronicles. Archaeological evidence (coins, pottery, settlement patterns) provides some corroboration but is unevenly distributed. Events in Britain, northern Europe, and beyond the Roman frontier are largely absent from the surviving record. The sources are heavily weighted toward the Mediterranean and Roman perspective.

graph_edges.note: The siege of Rome in 409 was part of the broader conflict that began with Alaric's march in 408.
The failed negotiations and continued siege in 409 led to the sack of Rome in 410.
The crossing of Vandals, Alans, and Sueves into Hispania in 409 led to the establishment of their kingdoms in the peninsula.
Constantine III's rule in 409 was challenged and eventually defeated in 411.
Theodosius II's consolidation of power in 409 led to the promulgation of the Theodosian Code in 438.
